annotations.label: Maximum likelihood estimation

anchor.snippet: is a method of estimating the parameters of an assumed probability distribution

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of maximum likelihood estimation or parameter estimators.

annotations.label: Likelihood function

anchor.snippet: which is called the likelihood function

annotations.note: No likelihood function L(θ;x) is defined in Mathlib; only densities/Radon-Nikodym derivatives exist.

anchors.snippet: which is called the likelihood function

annotations.label: Likelihood factorizes for independent variables

anchor.snippet: will be the product of univariate density functions

annotations.note: Mathlib characterizes independence by joint law factoring as a product of marginals, but not the likelihood-as-product statement.

anchors.snippet: will be the product of univariate density functions

annotations.label: MLE as maximizer of likelihood

anchor.snippet: to find the values of the model parameters that maximize the likelihood function over the parameter space

annotations.note: No argmax-of-likelihood construction exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: to find the values of the model parameters that maximize the likelihood function over the parameter space

annotations.label: Maximum likelihood estimate

anchor.snippet: is called the maximum likelihood estimate

annotations.note: No maximum likelihood estimate is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum likelihood estimator

anchor.snippet: then it is called the maximum likelihood estimator

annotations.note: No maximum likelihood estimator (as a measurable function of data) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sufficient condition for existence of MLE

anchor.snippet: A sufficient but not necessary condition for its existence is for the likelihood function to be continuous over a parameter space

annotations.note: The underlying extreme value theorem (a continuous function on a compact set attains its max) is formalized, but not its MLE-existence application.

annotations.label: Log-likelihood

anchor.snippet: called the log-likelihood

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the log-likelihood ratio (log of the Radon-Nikodym derivative), a closely related object but not the log-likelihood of a parameter.

annotations.label: Likelihood equations

anchor.snippet: known as the likelihood equations

annotations.note: Mathlib has Fermat's interior-extremum theorem (gradient vanishes at a local extremum), the general form of the first-order likelihood equations.

annotations.label: Negative semi-definite Hessian indicates local maximum

anchor.snippet: as this indicates local concavity

annotations.note: Mathlib has the one-dimensional second-derivative test, but not the multivariate negative-definite-Hessian criterion.

annotations.label: Constrained MLE optimization

anchor.snippet: means to find the maximum of the likelihood function subject to the constraint

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes constrained extrema via the Lagrange multipliers theorem, but not its MLE specialization.

annotations.label: Restricted likelihood equations via Lagrange

anchor.snippet: leads to the restricted likelihood equations

annotations.note: The Lagrange multiplier theorem yielding stationarity of a Lagrangian is formalized, but not the restricted likelihood equations specifically.

anchors.snippet: leads to the restricted likelihood equations

annotations.label: Lagrange multiplier test

anchor.snippet: known as the Lagrange multiplier test

annotations.note: The score/Lagrange-multiplier statistical hypothesis test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonparametric MLE via empirical likelihood

anchor.snippet: can be performed using the empirical likelihood

annotations.note: Empirical likelihood and nonparametric MLE are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: MLE as extremum estimator

anchor.snippet: is an extremum estimator obtained by maximizing

annotations.note: The framework of extremum estimators is not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: is an extremum estimator obtained by maximizing

annotations.label: Limiting properties of MLE

anchor.snippet: maximum likelihood estimation possesses a number of attractive limiting properties

annotations.note: No asymptotic theory of MLEs is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistency of the MLE

anchor.snippet: the maximum likelihood estimator is consistent

annotations.note: MLE consistency is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: MLE converges in probability

anchor.snippet: converges in probability to its true value

annotations.note: Convergence in probability (in measure) is defined in Mathlib, but its application to MLE consistency is not.

annotations.label: MLE converges almost surely

anchor.snippet: the estimator converges almost surely

annotations.note: Almost-sure convergence and the strong law of large numbers exist, but no almost-sure MLE consistency result.

annotations.label: Identification condition

anchor.snippet: different parameter values θ correspond to different distributions within the model

annotations.note: Model identifiability (injectivity of the parameter-to-distribution map) is not formalized as an MLE condition.

anchors.snippet: different parameter values θ correspond to different distributions within the model

annotations.label: Compactness condition

anchor.snippet: the parameter space Θ of the model is compact

annotations.note: Compactness exists as a general concept but not as a stated MLE-consistency hypothesis.

annotations.label: Continuity condition

anchor.snippet: is continuous in θ for almost all values of x

annotations.note: Continuity is a general Mathlib concept but this MLE-consistency regularity condition is not stated.

anchors.snippet: is continuous in θ for almost all values of x

annotations.label: Dominance condition

anchor.snippet: there exists D ( x ) integrable with respect to the distribution

annotations.note: Integrable dominating functions exist generally, but this MLE dominance condition is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: there exists D ( x ) integrable with respect to the distribution

annotations.label: Asymptotic normality of the MLE

anchor.snippet: the maximum likelihood estimator converges in distribution to a normal distribution

annotations.note: The 1D central limit theorem (convergence in distribution to a Gaussian) is formalized, but not the asymptotic normality of the MLE.

anchors.snippet: the maximum likelihood estimator converges in distribution to a normal distribution

annotations.label: Functional invariance of the MLE

anchor.snippet: is any transformation of

annotations.note: Functional invariance of the MLE is not formalized.

annotations.label: Profile likelihood

anchor.snippet: It maximizes the so-called profile likelihood

annotations.note: Profile likelihood is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivariance under data transformations

anchor.snippet: The MLE is also equivariant with respect to certain transformations of the data

annotations.note: MLE equivariance under data transformations is not formalized.

annotations.label: Log-normal MLE equals normal MLE of logs

anchor.snippet: the MLE parameters of the log-normal distribution are the same as those of the normal distribution fitted to the logarithm of the data

annotations.note: This log-normal MLE example is not formalized (no MLE, and no log-normal distribution).

annotations.label: Asymptotic efficiency reaches Cramér–Rao bound

anchor.snippet: asymptotically efficient, meaning that it reaches the Cramér–Rao bound

annotations.note: Neither Fisher information nor the Cramér–Rao bound appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bias zero up to order 1/√n

anchor.snippet: the bias of the maximum likelihood estimator is equal to zero up to the order

annotations.note: Higher-order bias expansions of the MLE are not formalized.

annotations.label: MLE has bias of order 1/n

anchor.section: Second-order efficiency after correction for bias
anchor.snippet: θ mle has bias of order

annotations.note: MLE bias-order results are not formalized.

annotations.label: Bias-corrected maximum likelihood estimator

anchor.section: Second-order efficiency after correction for bias
anchor.snippet: is called the bias-corrected maximum likelihood estimator

annotations.note: Bias-corrected MLEs are not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.section: Second-order efficiency after correction for bias
anchors.snippet: is called the bias-corrected maximum likelihood estimator

annotations.label: Second-order efficiency of bias-corrected estimator

anchor.section: Second-order efficiency after correction for bias
anchor.snippet: This bias-corrected estimator is second-order efficient

annotations.note: Second-order efficiency is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: MLE coincides with MAP under uniform prior

anchor.snippet: coincides with the most probable Bayesian estimator given a uniform prior distribution on the parameters

annotations.note: Neither MAP estimation nor its equivalence with the MLE under a uniform prior is formalized.

anchor.snippet: the Bayes Decision Rule is stated as

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Bayes risk in statistical decision theory, but not the Bayes decision rule itself as a stated optimal rule.

anchor.snippet: the Bayes Decision rule can be reformulated as

annotations.note: The reformulation of the Bayes rule under 0-1 loss (the MAP classifier) is not formalized.

anchor.section: Relation to minimizing Kullback–Leibler divergence and cross entropy
anchor.snippet: that maximizes the likelihood is asymptotically equivalent to finding the

annotations.note: KL divergence is defined in Mathlib, but the asymptotic equivalence of MLE with KL minimization is not.

annotations.label: MLE minimizes cross entropy

anchor.section: Relation to minimizing Kullback–Leibler divergence and cross entropy
anchor.snippet: the MLE is also asymptotically minimizing cross entropy

annotations.note: Cross entropy is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prediction bias of MLE

anchor.snippet: maximum likelihood prediction fails to propagate the uncertainty around the maximum likelihood parameter estimates into the prediction

annotations.note: Prediction bias of MLE-based prediction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Discrete uniform distribution MLE

anchor.snippet: of n is the number m on the drawn ticket

annotations.note: The uniform distribution exists in Mathlib but this MLE example is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: and so this is the maximum likelihood estimate for p

annotations.note: This finite-parameter MLE example for a coin is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bernoulli MLE, continuous parameter space

anchor.snippet: Thus the maximum likelihood estimator for p is

annotations.note: The Bernoulli/binomial distribution exists, but the MLE p̂ = x̄ is not formalized.

annotations.label: Generalized Bernoulli MLE s/n

anchor.snippet: which is the maximum likelihood estimator for any sequence of n Bernoulli trials resulting in s 'successes'

annotations.note: The MLE p̂ = s/n for Bernoulli trials is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: Formally we say that the maximum likelihood estimator for

annotations.note: The Gaussian distribution is formalized but its MLE example is not.

annotations.note: Unbiasedness of the sample-mean MLE is not formalized.

annotations.label: Variance MLE is biased

anchor.snippet: This means that the estimator

annotations.note: The bias of the sample-variance MLE is not formalized.

annotations.label: Normal log-likelihood at maximum

anchor.snippet: The normal log-likelihood at its maximum takes a particularly simple form

annotations.note: The closed form of the normal log-likelihood at its maximum is not formalized.

annotations.label: Independence via factorized density

anchor.snippet: are independent only if their joint probability density function is the product of the individual probability density functions

annotations.note: Mathlib characterizes independence by the joint law factoring as a product of marginal laws (the measure-level analogue of the density factorization).

annotations.label: Multivariate normal joint density

anchor.snippet: then follows a multivariate normal distribution given by

annotations.note: The multivariate Gaussian distribution with mean and covariance is defined in Mathlib for general finite dimension.

annotations.label: Bivariate normal joint density

anchor.snippet: In the bivariate case, the joint probability density function is given by

annotations.note: The bivariate normal is the 2-dimensional case of the formalized multivariate Gaussian, but the explicit ρ-parametrized density is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Multinomial joint probability

anchor.snippet: is called the multinomial and has the form

annotations.note: The multinomial coefficient is formalized, but the multinomial probability distribution itself is not.

annotations.label: Constrained multinomial MLE via Lagrange

anchor.snippet: the most natural estimate is derived

annotations.note: This worked multinomial MLE example via Lagrange multipliers is not formalized.

annotations.label: Likelihood equations not solvable explicitly

anchor.snippet: cannot be solved explicitly for an estimator

annotations.note: This is an informal observation with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Iterative updating formula

anchor.snippet: algorithms based on an updating formula of the form

annotations.note: Generic numerical optimization update schemes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient descent method

anchor.snippet: Gradient descent method requires to calculate the gradient at the r -th iteration

annotations.note: Gradient descent is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newton–Raphson step

anchor.snippet: is the inverse of the Hessian matrix of the log-likelihood function

annotations.note: Mathlib's Newton map is for polynomial root-finding over rings, not the inverse-Hessian optimization step.

annotations.label: Berndt–Hall–Hall–Hausman algorithm

anchor.snippet: approximates the Hessian with the outer product of the expected gradient

annotations.note: The BHHH algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasi-Newton secant updates

anchor.snippet: use more elaborate secant updates to give approximation of Hessian matrix

annotations.note: Quasi-Newton secant updates are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Davidon–Fletcher–Powell formula

anchor.snippet: DFP formula finds a solution that is symmetric, positive-definite and closest to the current approximate value of second-order derivative

annotations.note: The DFP formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: DFP formula finds a solution that is symmetric, positive-definite and closest to the current approximate value of second-order derivative

annotations.label: BFGS algorithm

anchor.snippet: BFGS also gives a solution that is symmetric and positive-definite

annotations.note: The BFGS algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BFGS convergence condition

anchor.snippet: BFGS method is not guaranteed to converge unless the function has a quadratic Taylor expansion near an optimum

annotations.note: BFGS convergence results are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fisher scoring algorithm

anchor.snippet: to replace the Hessian with the Fisher information matrix

annotations.note: Fisher scoring is not formalized; Mathlib has no Fisher information matrix.

annotations.label: Quasi-Newton convergence to stationary point

anchor.snippet: may converge to a stationary point that is not necessarily a local or global maximum

annotations.note: Convergence behavior of quasi-Newton iterations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wilks' theorem

anchor.snippet: now called Wilks' theorem

annotations.note: Wilks' theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymptotic chi-squared distribution of log-likelihood error

anchor.snippet: is asymptotically χ 2 -distributed

annotations.note: The asymptotic chi-squared distribution of the likelihood-ratio statistic is not formalized in Mathlib.
